quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 2, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Limiting Reactants (Continued)
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Limiting Reactant Calculations, Stoichiometry, Mole Ratios, Identifying Excess Reactants

questions.question: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃. 7 moles of N₂ react with 18 moles of H₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio N₂:H₂ = 1:3
For 7 moles N₂, 21 moles H₂ are required. Only 18 moles H₂ are available.
Therefore, H₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Ca + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + H₂. 20 g of Ca reacts with 5 g of H₂O. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Ca = 40 g/mol, H₂O = 18 g/mol
Moles Ca = 20/40 = 0.5 mol
Moles H₂O = 5/18 = 0.278 mol
Mole ratio Ca:H₂O = 1:2
For 0.5 mol Ca, 1.0 mol H₂O is required. Only 0.278 mol H₂O is available.
Therefore, H₂O is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. 12 g of Mg reacts with 16 g of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Mg = 24 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles Mg = 12/24 = 0.5 mol
Moles O₂ = 16/32 = 0.5 mol
Mole ratio Mg:O₂ = 2:1
For 0.5 mol O₂, 1.0 mol Mg is required. Only 0.5 mol Mg is available.
Therefore, Mg is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. 16 g of CH₄ reacts with 64 g of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass CH₄ = 16 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol
Moles CH₄ = 16/16 = 1 mol
Moles O₂ = 64/32 = 2 mol
The reaction requires a 1:2 mole ratio exactly.
Both reactants are present in exact stoichiometric amounts and are completely consumed.

questions.question: 2Al + 3Cl₂ → 2AlCl₃. 27 g of Al reacts with 106 g of Cl₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Al = 27 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Al = 27/27 = 1 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 106/71 = 1.49 mol
Mole ratio Al:Cl₂ = 2:3, so for 1 mol Al, (3/2) × 1 = 1.5 mol Cl₂ is required.
1.49 mol Cl₂ is available, which is slightly less than required.
Therefore, Al is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2C₂H₆ + 7O₂ → 4CO₂ + 6H₂O. 6 moles of C₂H₆ react with 40 moles of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio C₂H₆:O₂ = 2:7 = 1:3.5
For 6 moles C₂H₆, 6 × 3.5 = 21 moles O₂ are required. 40 moles O₂ are available, so O₂ is in excess.
For 40 moles O₂, 40 ÷ 3.5 = 11.43 moles C₂H₆ are required. Only 6 moles C₂H₆ are available.
Therefore, C₂H₆ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: H₂ + Br₂ → 2HBr. 4 moles of H₂ react with 5 moles of Br₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
The reaction is 1:1.
For 4 moles H₂, 4 moles Br₂ are required. 5 moles Br₂ are available, so Br₂ is in excess.
For 5 moles Br₂, 5 moles H₂ are required. Only 4 moles H₂ are available.
Therefore, H₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl. 10 g of Na reacts with 20 g of Cl₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Na = 23 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Na = 10/23 = 0.435 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 20/71 = 0.282 mol
Mole ratio Na:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 0.282 mol Cl₂, 0.564 mol Na are required. Only 0.435 mol Na are available.
Therefore, Na is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. 160 g of Fe₂O₃ reacts with 90 g of CO. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Fe₂O₃ = 160 g/mol, CO = 28 g/mol
Moles Fe₂O₃ = 160/160 = 1 mol
Moles CO = 90/28 = 3.21 mol
Mole ratio Fe₂O₃:CO = 1:3
For 1 mol Fe₂O₃, 3 mol CO are required. 3.21 mol CO are available, so CO is in excess.
Therefore, Fe₂O₃ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. 4 moles of H₂ react with 1 mole of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio H₂:O₂ = 2:1
For 4 moles H₂, 2 moles O₂ are required. Only 1 mole O₂ is available.
Therefore, O₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. 12 g of Mg reacts with 6 g of HCl. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Mg = 24 g/mol, HCl = 36.5 g/mol
Moles Mg = 12/24 = 0.5 mol
Moles HCl = 6/36.5 = 0.164 mol
Mole ratio Mg:HCl = 1:2
For 0.5 mol Mg, 1.0 mol HCl is required. Only 0.164 mol HCl is available.
Therefore, HCl is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: 2C₄H₁₀ + 13O₂ → 8CO₂ + 10H₂O. 2 moles of C₄H₁₀ react with 13 moles of O₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio C₄H₁₀:O₂ = 2:13
For 2 moles C₄H₁₀, 13 moles O₂ are required. Exactly 13 moles O₂ are available.
Both reactants are present in exact stoichiometric amounts and are completely consumed.

questions.question: 2Al + 3Cl₂ → 2AlCl₃. 54 g of Al reacts with 106 g of Cl₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Al = 27 g/mol, Cl₂ = 71 g/mol
Moles Al = 54/27 = 2 mol
Moles Cl₂ = 106/71 = 1.49 mol
Mole ratio Al:Cl₂ = 2:3
For 2 mol Al, 3 mol Cl₂ are required. Only 1.49 mol Cl₂ is available.
Therefore, Cl₂ is the limiting reactant.

questions.question: Zn + S → ZnS. 65 g of Zn reacts with 32 g of S. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Molar mass Zn = 65 g/mol, S = 32 g/mol
Moles Zn = 65/65 = 1 mol
Moles S = 32/32 = 1 mol
The reaction is 1:1, so both reactants are present in exact stoichiometric amounts.
Both are completely consumed.

questions.question: 2K + Cl₂ → 2KCl. 10 moles of K react with 3 moles of Cl₂. The limiting reactant is:
questions.explanation:
Mole ratio K:Cl₂ = 2:1
For 10 moles K, 5 moles Cl₂ are required. Only 3 moles Cl₂ are available.
Therefore, Cl₂ is the limiting reactant.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered limiting reactant calculations! 🧪⚖️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review mole ratios and how to identify the limiting reactant.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on comparing required moles with available moles.
scoreMessages.0: Please review stoichiometry, mole ratios, and limiting reactant concepts.
